best_contact.decision_maker: ## [Be the CMO Everyone Wants to Work With](https://kellblog.com/2026/03/21/how-to-be-the-cmo-everyone-wants-to-work-with/)
I’m a big fan of Dave Gerhardt and the [Exit Five](https://www.exitfive.com/) marketing community he has built. While I have always liked the idea of peer-networking communities
best_contact.department: I think they change from vitamin to painkiller in times of rapid change. Why? Because the playbooks haven’t been written yet.

best_contact.email: you can find scores of blogs — including mine — that talk about how to run SaaS businesses
best_contact.job_title: and how to produce and interpret SaaS metrics. Twenty years ago virtually none of that existed. You needed to figure it out. And one of the best ways to figure it out was to spend time with other people who were doing the same figuring.

best_contact.decision_maker: There’s a time for timeless wisdom and there’s a time for talking to other people who are doing the same thing as you are
best_contact.department: right now. I think AI and the massive disruption it creates in marketing — from content to workflow to performance marketing to analytics — means it’s an awesome time to join a marketing peer-networking community.

best_contact.decision_maker: I tell this to literally every CMO I work with:
* Come to me for timeless wisdom (if not fleeting opinions).
* Join Exit Five (or equivalent) to talk to peers who face the same challenges you do
That’s not to say that following thought leaders isn’t a great tactic
best_contact.department: too. I keep an eye on Emily Kramer
best_contact.linkedin_url: and Jon Miller. And I can’t wait for Rand Fishkin’s rumored new book on Zero-Click Marketing. If there are other marketing thought leaders you think I should be following

best_contact.decision_maker: All this is why I was thrilled when Dave Gerhardt invited me to speak at Exit Five’s Marketing Leadership Retreat on March 19-20 in Phoenix. Given the above
best_contact.department: I knew I wasn’t going to be the person delivering fresh-from-the-trenches information on AI tools and methods. The audience is 100x more qualified than I am to do that.

best_contact.decision_maker: So what did I want to offer up instead? Some timeless wisdom. Specifically
best_contact.department: timeless wisdom not just on how to successfully do the CMO job
best_contact.email: but on how to be the CMO everyone wants to work with.

best_contact.decision_maker: Why that topic? And bear in mind it takes a lot for me to pick a title that ends with a preposition. Because I thought it captured the key to success in an important way.
* **It’s not just about doing the job**. Yes
best_contact.department: that’s quite hard already but if you only focus on that you ironically increase your odds of becoming a statistic.

best_contact.decision_maker: * **It’s not just about keeping the job**. And yes
best_contact.email: a big part of that is simply remembering to market marketing.

best_contact.decision_maker: * **It’s about helping the boss do their job** which not only increases your strategic value
best_contact.department: but makes you more “sticky” in your role.

best_contact.decision_maker: * It’s about doing all that **while being the CMO that everyone wants to work with** (EW2WW)
Why does that last point matter?

best_contact.department: so a strong [internal customer service](https://kellblog.com/2020/04/26/marketing-exists-to-make-sales-easier/) orientation never hurts.

best_contact.decision_maker: * Being the CMO EW2WW **helps you keep your job**. With a median tenure of 18-24 months
best_contact.department: this should never be too far from a CMO’s mind. Belt and suspenders.

best_contact.decision_maker: * If _everyone_ wants to work with you
best_contact.department: **it helps you find your next job**. Board members and recruiters — your top two job sources after peers — will seek you out when the time comes.

best_contact.decision_maker: * It might well **help you get promoted to COO or CEO**. If everyone wants to work with you
best_contact.department: they might well give you a shot at the next level.

best_contact.decision_maker: * **It frames things as a positioning problem**. And we know a lot about solving positioning problems
best_contact.department: working backwards from a desired result.
I’m saying that CMOs should want to position themselves as the CMO EW2WW.
here are the slides from the presentation
best_contact.full_name: and downloadable [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1vYxyNiYkkJbL8O2CMzHrP71ubqDNqtAK/view?usp=sharing). I had a fairly miserable time in Gamma building them so apologies for the upside-down funnel
best_contact.phone: the absence of copyright notice and slide numbers). There is only so much time I’m willing to spend explaining to a chatbot what expression to put on the reflected image of a face in a mirror. And once you drop into PowerPoint to make changes
best_contact.seniority: there seems to be no going back.

best_contact.decision_maker: Thanks again to Dave for having me and to Allison Saxon for working with me to make it happen.
## [Why I’m Not Worried About Running Out of Work in the Age of AI](https://kellblog.com/2026/03/19/why-im-not-worried-about-running-out-of-work-in-the-age-of-ai/)
When the auto industry was being decimated and jobs offshored to Japan
best_contact.department: many of us instinctively reached for our college economics textbook and started thinking about fish and coconuts — the example Paul Samuelson used to explain David Ricardo’s theory of [comparative advantage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparative_advantage) in international trade.
if Japan is better at making cars than we are
best_contact.email: then they should make cars and we should do other things. It’ll be rough for the displaced auto workers but it’s merely a transition period and
best_contact.job_title: it’ll be a win/win for both countries and we’ll all be better off in the end.

best_contact.decision_maker: If you put aside the many concerns with basing policy solely on comparative advantage (e.g.
best_contact.linkedin_url: you could at least get a good night’s sleep.

best_contact.department: as I pointed out in my [2026 predictions post](https://kellblog.com/2026/01/22/kellblog-predictions-for-2026/)
best_contact.email: while it’s painful in the short term
best_contact.full_name: we typically don’t miss the jobs displaced by technology.

best_contact.decision_maker: _I don’t think anyone misses elevator operators
best_contact.department: a job that peaked in the 1950s and is all but extinct today. The same is true of switchboard operators
best_contact.seniority: and slubber doffers. Yet all these jobs were automated away. While I’m sure the transition was rough for any given elevator operator
best_contact_id: as a society we don’t miss them. Most coal miners work hard so their children won’t have to be coal miners._

best_contact.decision_maker: It sucks for **them** — the displaced workers — of course
best_contact.department: but collectively it’s good for us. Or
best_contact.job_title: but the costs are often geographically concentrated and painful (e.g.
best_contact.linkedin_url: the Rust Belt). If you want to know what it **feels** like to be on the other end of this displacement
company.blog_url: [The River](https://kellblog.com/2023/02/18/is-a-dream-a-lie-if-it-dont-come-true-founders-aspirations-and-company-potential/). That’s what it feels like in a dying town. That’s the darkness on the edge of town.

best_contact.decision_maker: The pain is real and while occupational transfers are a solution
best_contact.department: some are easier than others. An autoworker could transition to another manufacturing job (though often at lower wages) but would be unlikely to become a mechanical engineer. For the economy in general
best_contact.email: jobs transition from one sector to another. For any given person
best_contact.full_name: one displacement could be the end of the line.

best_contact.decision_maker: Another excerpt from the predictions post:
_Let’s remember where the word Luddite comes from. Today
best_contact.department: we use the word to describe people opposed to new technology. But the Luddites were part of a**labor** movement; they didn’t destroy machines because they were technophobes
best_contact.email: they destroyed them to protect jobs. So the transitions can be rough — and come with social unrest — when jobs are automated away. But we still don’t miss the jobs they fought for._

best_contact.department: with our safe emotional distance from a 1970s auto worker
best_contact.email: what would we have wanted to tell them at the start of the disruption? I’d say:

best_contact.department: it’s a trend. This is not a problem you wait out.

best_contact.decision_maker: * You could try to fight your way through this
best_contact.department: but the odds will not be forever in your favor.
ride this one to the beach and go retire.

best_contact.decision_maker: * Switching early is better than switching late. The goal is to get onto the next horse before everyone else realizes the race has moved.
The popular horse-switching fantasy answer is retraining. “Go back to school and become an engineer.” In theory
best_contact.email: rarely. The jump from an assembly-line worker to an engineer requires years of schooling and a different educational foundation.

best_contact.decision_maker: The real move for the autoworker was sideways
None of these are glamorous answers
best_contact.department: but they share an important property: they’re adjacent to the skill set the person already has. They’re realistic.
without the emotional distance we have from a 1970s auto worker
best_contact.email: let’s try applying the same thinking to ourselves. One way to do that is to imagine what someone from the year 2070 might write back to us.

best_contact.decision_maker: They might start the same way.

best_contact.department: it’s a trend. Don’t assume this wave of AI capability will conveniently stall just below your job description. You can try to fight your way through it
best_contact.email: but the odds will not be forever in your favor. The job you’re defending may still exist
best_contact.full_name: but the number of people needed to do it may fall dramatically.

best_contact.decision_maker: They might add something else we don’t particularly like hearing: if you’re late in your career
best_contact.department: you may be able to ride this out. Large organizations change slowly and adoption curves usually take time. But I think less so in this case — it might prove to be a short ride. If you’re early or mid-career
best_contact.email: you should be thinking about switching horses.

best_contact.decision_maker: But here the analogy gets interesting.
The auto worker in 1975 often had a high-school education and a skill set tightly tied to the factory floor. The realistic moves were sideways into adjacent trades.

best_contact.email: is already highly trained. Many have college or graduate degrees and have spent years building transferable skills: writing

best_contact.department: knowledge workers should be much easier to redeploy than auto workers ever were.
this all hits different because **this time it’s us**. That’s where we lose the emotional distance
best_contact.email: and why the people trying to scare us sound so convincing.

best_contact.decision_maker: Citrini’s recent [thought experiment](https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/2028gic) imagines a negative feedback loop with no natural brake. As they describe it:

best_contact.job_title: margin pressure pushed firms to invest more in AI

best_contact.department: the problem isn’t sudden unemployment so much as a slow erosion of the income base that supports the modern consumer economy. Highly paid white-collar workers lose their jobs
best_contact.full_name: compress wages across the service sector
best_contact.job_title: and reduce spending at the same time.

best_contact.decision_maker: The one thing I find most missing from the AI future analyses I’ve read is a simple realization: in my experience
best_contact.email: always more work yet to do.**

best_contact.decision_maker: This relates to how I’ve always thought about the total available market (TAM) for business intelligence (BI). Over the decades I’ve worked in and around BI
best_contact.department: I’ve been asked countless times whether the market was saturated or saturating.

best_contact.decision_maker: My answer was always the same.
One day I’ll go to a cocktail party and ask everyone in the room: “Does everybody have all the information they need to make data-informed management decisions?” If everyone says yes
best_contact.department: then on that day the BI market will be saturated.

best_contact.decision_maker: But that day strikes me as a long way off. It did 40 years ago and it still does today. We have accomplished so
best_contact.department: so much in BI and analytics. But there is still so much more to do.

best_contact.decision_maker: Since no AI post would be complete without a reference to [Jevons Paradox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jevons_paradox)
best_contact.department: let’s invoke it here: When technology makes something cheaper or more efficient
best_contact.email: we tend to use more of it
best_contact.full_name: not less. When steam engines became more efficient
best_contact.job_title: coal consumption didn’t fall — it exploded because steam power became economical in far more applications. The same dynamic has repeated itself with computing
best_contact.location: and bandwidth. Every time technology dramatically increases our ability to do something
best_contact.phone: we don’t run out of work. We discover many more things worth doing.

best_contact.decision_maker: The vast majority of management and strategic consulting today is about focus. And the reason focus matters so much is simple: execution bandwidth is limited. Organizations can only get so many things right.
So managers spend an enormous amount of time cutting
best_contact.linkedin_url: if we had to list the one key to business success
best_contact.location: it’s focus. Why? Because there is so much work that we could do. So many strategies that we can imagine. So many things that we could do to help our customers.

best_contact.decision_maker: It’s not that there’s _only a little_ more work to do. There is an unfathomable amount of work yet to be done. The job of management is pruning that list down to something the organization can actually execute.

Which is why the idea that we’re somehow going to run out of work strikes me as absurd. It feels like a theory written by people who haven’t actually spent much time doing the work in the first place — serving customers
best_contact.email: and running businesses. There is _always_ more that could be done.

best_contact.decision_maker: There’s also an irony here. Increasingly
best_contact.department: the people who say SaaS will stand for “service as a software” are the same people saying AI will massively disrupt employment. But when you sell service-as-a-software you are definitionally in the solutions business
best_contact.full_name: not merely delivering data and algorithms. And when you look at your customers through the lens of “problems yet to be solved
best_contact.job_title: ” you quickly discover that the list is very

best_contact.department: I’m not particularly worried about us running out of work to do.

best_contact.decision_maker: I do think the transition could be difficult
best_contact.department: especially if you’re not ready for it. So
best_contact.email: given everything we’ve said thus far
best_contact.full_name: here’s the advice I’d give to a knowledge worker heading into this transition.

best_contact.decision_maker: You want to be driving the tools
best_contact.department: not driven by them. Aggressively learn AI. Be the person who knows the most about solving problems using AI tools — integrating them

# The fastest way to finance software and hardware
Choose Capchase as your financing partner to close deals faster and increase your earnings. Approve your buyer, configure your offer, and win your deal in minutes.
[Book a call](https://www.capchase.com/demo)
Trusted by over 10,000 vendors & buyers
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68c2bfd6294531eb547b806e_gradient-1.webp)
### Buyers expect payment options
82% of U.S. companies use financing for equipment and software. If you don’t offer it, you risk losing the deal.
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68c2c0d038469984e1c349d7_image-2.webp)
### Financing is often the roadblock
Clunky, slow, and unreliable processes stall sales. Done right, financing accelerates deals and keeps you in control.
### Capchase makes financing easier
Close high-velocity or high-complexity transactions in minutes, not weeks. Reseller partner approved.
## Experience the Capchase advantage
Embedded vendor financing that delivers faster approvals, easy deal configurations, and real-time deal status–right where you work.
For Sales
### Close more deals at a higher value
Use flexible payment options as a competitive advantage. Whether your customer prefers monthly, quarterly, or extended net terms - give them the choice they want.
[Learn more](https://www.capchase.com/department/sales)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68cc2f18bce12671fa026ec0_laptop-new.webp)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/67b486ac339ddec78540d46d_Card-neww.png)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/67adf0f761a0d053b55105ad_Finance-1.webp)
For Finance
### Get paid upfront without slowing sales
Offer payment flexibility while still getting paid upfront on every deal—including multi-year and reseller deals—eliminating billing headaches and putting an end to chasing payments.
[Learn more](https://www.capchase.com/department/finance)
## Win-win for your business, partners, and customers
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68c2da91024544ae491cae81_webp.webp)
### Vendors & Manufacturers
Get paid upfront, empower customers with flexible payments, and leave the collections burden to us. Partner channel approved.
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68c2dabce8165ab45ce7b7dd_layout.webp)
### Reseller Partners
Cut closing from weeks to minutes with easy financing, seamless checkout, and faster payouts.
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/68c2daef4a17b8835cbdcbc1_buyer.webp)
### Buyers
Allow your customers to optimize their cash flow while still purchasing the tech they need to be successful.
## Everything you need to simplify B2B financing
Use Capchase from within your sales team's daily tools. Create a white-labeled captive financing program. Enjoy a faster, more responsive, and better experience.  

[Learn more](https://www.capchase.com/features)
### Easy deal configuration  

Generate payment options to best fit your deal including term length, payment frequency, net terms, and subsidy—calculating pricing in real-time.
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/679b7b12129d16826d688902_PL-visual.svg)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6146543ab50f167ae088b201/679b7bc368ed600e8a5ded7f_Vendor-PL.svg)
### Instant buyer qualification
Approve your customers in advance—in just minutes not weeks—so you can offer financing confidently.
